content.full_text:
Lawrence A. Haun
Roser A. Brown [$ i;
Lew Orrrcrs or
., ,f{cvcn and One-half South Washin8on Strclt
\ I 'il .'il J I 
Post offic. Box 1056
Sonora, California 95370
Tclephonc (209) 532-1958 Tclccopicr (209) 532-1963
February 72 | ]-997
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral counsel
Fair PoIitical Practices Conmission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804
RE: Request for Advice
Dear Mr. Hal labrin:
This is to request specific advice concerning my clients'
duties under the Political- Reform Act of :-9'74, as amended, ('tThe
Act'r) pursuant to Government Code section 83114(b). My cLients
are BKK Corporation, Greenfield Environmental and their
affiliated entities and employees.
OUESTIONS PRESENTED
l-. Who is the lobbyist employer to be identified on Form
635 r^/here an rrin-house" employee of two af f il, j-ated companies is a
registered lobbyist and a portion of her salary is paid by each
affillated entity?
2. !{here a lobbyist is an employee of two cornpanies v/j-th a
portion of her salary paid by each, and she lobbies only for
these t$/o companies, is she a lobbying firm or an employee of a
lobbyist ernployer for registration and reporting purposes?
FACTS
Kathy Fletcher is a new Iobbyist and an in-house employee of
both creenfield Environnental and BKK Corporation. She is paid a
salary and has deductions taken out for payroll taxes. Ms.
Fletcher is etigible for the normaf benefits accorded to
employees. She has all the custonary earmarks of an emplovee.
The hours, conditions and manner of accomplishing her tasks are
set by her enpl-oyer. Ms. Fletcher does not operate her own
business, has filed no fictitious 
business name statement and
works out of the Greenfield offices in Sacramento. Ms. Fletcher
is an employee, not an independent contractor.
Ms. Fletcher is paid by payrol-l check drawn on the payroll
account of Greenf iel,d Environmental. However, an affiliated
entity, BKK Corporation, reimburses creenfield for a portion of


Scott Hallabrin
February ]-2, L99l
Page 2
Ms. Fletcher's salary. 
The cornpanies each reinburse Ms. Fletcher
for her expenses. In turn, Ms. Fletcher is registered to lobby
on behalf of both BKK corporation and Greenfield Environmental.
BKK Corporation and Greenf iel-d Environmental are affil"iated
entities within the meaning of 2 cal-ifornia Code of Regulations
section 18428(b). The companies have comnon sharehoLders,
directors and managers and file Major Donor Campaign Statements
aggregating their contributions. 
The lobbying efforts of both
companies are coordinated. Kathy Fletcher v'rorks at the direction
of the comnon managenent. Her employment agreenent states, arnong
other things, that she will:
Monitor aII state leqislative and
adnj.nistrative agency proposed 1alrs and
regulations affectinqr the business activlties
of Greenfield and BKK.
Represent Greenfield, and if requested, BKK
corporation, before the State Legislature and
State adninistrative agencies
When it carne tirne for BKK and Greenf iel-d to register with
the Secretary of state as lobbyist empl-oyers, the FPPC technical
assistance staff advised Ms. Fletcher and me that she should
register as a lobbying firrn and her ernployers need not register
at all, but should continue filing 
Form 635rs as a lobbyj-st
employer. We have followed this advice but wish to receive
formal written advice because we are confused about the authority
for the advice we were given.
DI SCUSS ION
A t'Lobbyist Enpfoyert is defined in Government code secti-on
82039.5 to mean, among other things, rrany person other than a
lobbying f irrn who:
(a) Emplovs one or rnore lobbyists for
economi-c consideration. . . .
(b) contracts for the services of a lobbying
firrn for econonic consideration. . . . (ernphasis
added . )
In contrast, a 'tLobbying Firrnrr is defined in Government Code
section 82038.5 to mean:
any business entitv, including an individual
contract lobbyist, which meets either of the
following criteria:


Scott HaIIabrin
February L2, L99L
Page 3
(1) The business entity receives or becones
entitled to receive any compensation... for
the purpose of influencing legislative or
administrative action on behal-f of any other
person, and any partner, or^/ner, officer, or
employee of the business entity is a
lobbyist.
(2) The business entity receives or becomes
entitled to receive any compensation. . . to
cornmunicate directly with any elective state
official, 
agency official , or legislative
official 
for the purpose of infJ,uencing
legislative, or adninj-strative action on
behalf of any other person, if a substantial
or regular portion of the activities for
which the business entitv receives
compensation is for the purpose of
influencing legislative or administrative
action. (Ernphasis added. )
Significantl,y, only business entities may be lobbying f j,rns
as the terrn is defined in the Act. A rtBusiness Entity't is
defined to be,
any organizatj,on or enterprise operated for
profit, including but not linited to a
proprietorship, partnership, firm, business
trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation
or association. (covernnent code section
82005. )
Kathy Ffetcher is an ernployee, not a business entity. 
The
clear meaning of the statute can lead to only one conclusion,
that an employee of a lobbyist employer cannot be a lobbying
firm. 
However clear the statute, there are additional reasons
why Kathy Fletcher cannot be a lobbying firm.
Conrnission Regulation 18238.5 further defines a lobbying
firn and continues the requirement that a lobbying firm be a
business entity with a partner, orrrner, officer, or employee of
the business entitv v,rho engages in direct communication. Thus,
both the statute and the regulation limit the definition of
lobbying firm to certain t'business entitiesr', as defined. The
very concept of Lobbying firm suggests sornething other than an
individual employee. But, there is more.
The FPPC fnformation l"lanua1 on Lobbying Discfosure
Provisions of The Political Reform Act (1988 Ed.,ttThe Manual-")
describes what is a lobbying firm. 
There are a number of


Scott HaLlabrin
February L2, ).99]-
Page 4
exanples to illustrate the defi,nition. 
The only example of an
employee concerns an employee of a lobbvinq firm who rece.ives
payments directly from each of two different lobbyi-st employers.
In this situation, the lobbyist is an 'rindividual contract
lobbyist" who is required to register as a lobbying finn.
The example is significantly different from Kathy Eletcher's
situation in at least two irnportant respects. First, Ms.
Fletcher is not an employee of a lobbying f irrn. She is an
employee of a lobbyist employer. Second, the FPPC has already
advised us that BKK corporation and Greenfield are a sinsle
lobbyist employer hrho must file as such on a sinqle Forn 635.
Thus, Ms. Fletcherrs situation differs from the example for the
additional reason that she receives payments directly from a
single lobbyist employer.
Another example in the ManuaL is helpful for vrhat it
irnplies. The fourth example on page 3 of the Manuaf describes
rrBarbara Brownrt (no relation) as a lobbying firm where she has a
contract with a corporation but,
rrThe corporation incurs no payroll taxes for
Bro\,rn and does not provide her with health,
retirement or other benefits. 
Even though
Bro!,rn has no other clients or enpLoyers,
because she is not an emplovee of the
corporation, she is an "individual contract
lobbyistfl and must register as a ]-obbying
firm and file periodic reports . " ( ernphas is
added . )
Kathy Fletcher is an ernplovee of the corporation which does
incur payroll taxes and does offer her ernployee benefits.
Therefore, by necessary implication, Kathy Fletcher is not an
t'individual contract lobbyisttt and should not be required to
register as a Iobbying firn.
FinaIIy, the forrns devised by the FPPC clearly show the
Commission never intended for enployees to register and report as
lobbyj-ng firms. There is no place on Form 625 (Report of
Lobbying Firm) to disclose such things as payroll tax, health or
retirement benefits or other normal enployee benefits. 
These are
payments which are normally rnade only to empfoyees, not
independent contractors. while the forrn has no place for wages,
salary or benefits, it does capture such norrnaf independent
contractor payments as rrfees and retainers.rl
If an enpl-oyee is required to report as a lobbying firn,
significant problems elnerge. For example, is the employee
required to report gross or net salary on Form 625? If payroll


scott HaI Iabrin
February 72, 199L
Paqe 5
taxes and benefits are payments recej.ved in connection with
lobbyingr activity, 
where are they to be reported? Since Kathy
Ffetcher is not a firm or a business entity, who are the
rrpartners, owners, officers or employeestr of the lobbying firm
v,/ho are to be identified on Part I of Form 625?
CONCIJUSION
The Political Reform Act, the Regulations promulgated by the
Fair Political, Practices Cornmission, the llanuaf issued by the
FPPC and the forms are in agreement that a lobbying firm cannot
be an individual employee of a Lobbyist employer. The lobbying
firrn must be a rrbusiness entityrt, as defined, or an individual
contract lobbyist. 
s j,nce Kathy Fletcher is neither a business
entity nor an individual contract lobbyj,st, she should not be
required to register as a lobbying f irrn.
We respectfully ask the commission to render formal advice
consistent with this analysis. The effect of this advice would
be to permit Kathy Ftetcher to file peri,odic lobbyist reports and
to require BKK Corporation and Greenf j,eld Envi-ronmental to
register as a lobbyist employer. Kathy Fletcher would withdraw
her prior registration as a lobbyinqr f irrn. 
BKK and Greenfield
woutd continue to file periodic Form 635rs.
The results of the advice we seek are consistent with the
spirit, 
intent and literal 
meaning of the Political Reforn Act,
the Regulations, the Forns and the Manuaf. No significant
inforrnation wilt be lost, but Ms. Fletcher wil,1 be saved the
usel-ess task of fiting redundant reports as both a lobbyist and a
lobbying firn.
Brown

Y*rs,
[3"r*---

sections.question:
1. Who is the lobbyist employer to be identified on Form 635 where an "in-house" employee of two affiliated companies is a registered lobbyist and a portion of her salary is paid by each affiliated entity?

2. Where a lobbyist is an employee of two companies with a portion of her salary paid by each, and she lobbies only for these two companies, is she a lobbying firm or an employee of a lobbyist employer for registration and reporting purposes?

sections.question_synthetic: The requestor seeks FPPC advice on whether Kathy Fletcher, an in-house employee of two affiliated companies (BKK Corporation and Greenfield Environmental) who is paid partially by each entity and registered to lobby for both, should be classified as a lobbying firm or as an employee of a lobbyist employer for registration purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an incoming REQUEST for advice, not an FPPC response letter. The requestor provides extensive discussion and argument in addition to questions, seeking formal written confirmation of prior informal technical assistance advice. Document is dated February 12, 1991 (though header shows 1997). No formal FPPC conclusion/response is present in this document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Who is the lobbyist employer to be identified on Form 635 where an "in-house" employee of two affiliated companies is a registered lobbyist and a portion of her salary is paid by each affiliated entity?

2. Where a lobbyist is an employee of two companies with a portion of her salary paid by each, and she lobbies only for these two companies, is she a lobbying firm or an employee of a lobbyist employer for registration and reporting purposes?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from attorney Roser A. Brown seeking formal FPPC advice regarding the proper registration and reporting classification for Kathy Fletcher, an in-house lobbyist employee of two affiliated entities. The letter argues that Fletcher should register as a lobbyist employee rather than as a lobbying firm, supported by statutory analysis, FPPC regulations, manuals, and form design.
